questions.question: You wrap a blog post title, author byline, and publication date inside a <div>. A screen reader user reports they can't distinguish the article header from the rest of the page. What's the most likely fix?

options.text: Replace the <div> with an <article> containing a <header>. Semantic elements expose document structure to assistive technology automatically.
Add role="heading" to the <div>. This tells screen readers to treat the entire block as a heading region.
Apply aria-label="article header" to the <div>. ARIA labels are the only way to convey structure to screen readers.

Add a
tag before the section so screen readers detect the visual gap and announce a new region.

questions.explanation: <article> and <header> provide native semantic landmarks that assistive technology recognizes without extra ARIA.

questions.question: A form has two text inputs but no visible labels — only placeholder text. QA reports the form fails WCAG compliance. What's the root issue?

options.text: Placeholders disappear on focus, leaving no persistent label. Screen readers may not announce the field purpose. Use <label> elements associated via htmlFor.
Placeholders are read by all screen readers identically to labels. The issue is likely the placeholder color contrast ratio being too low.
WCAG requires title attributes on inputs, not labels. Placeholders only fail because title is missing.
The form needs a <fieldset> wrapper to pass WCAG. Individual field labeling isn't required if the fieldset has a <legend>.

questions.explanation: Placeholders are not accessible labels — they vanish on input and aren't reliably announced. <label> with for/htmlFor is required.

questions.question: You set a flex container with justify-content: space-between, but items still cluster on the left. The container has a fixed width of 300px and two items are each 150px wide. Why doesn't space-between work?

options.text: The items exactly fill the container (150+150=300px), leaving zero free space. space-between distributes free space, so there's nothing to distribute.
space-between only works with three or more items. With two items, it defaults to flex-start behavior.
justify-content doesn't affect flex items; it controls text alignment within each item. Use align-items instead.
Flex items ignore fixed widths. They auto-shrink to content size, so space-between sees them as smaller and bunches them left.

questions.explanation: space-between distributes remaining free space. When items fill 100% of the container, there is zero free space to distribute.

questions.question: You set height: 100% on a div to make it fill the viewport, but it collapses to the height of its content. The parent is <body>. What's happening?

options.text: Percentage height requires the parent to have an explicit height. <body> and <html> default to auto height — set html, body { height: 100% } or use 100vh.
height: 100% only works on block elements. The div is likely set to display: inline somewhere in the cascade.
Modern browsers ignore percentage heights for security reasons. Use min-height: 100% which bypasses this restriction.
The body has default margin collapsing that absorbs the div's height. Remove margin: 0 on body to fix it.

questions.explanation: Percentage height resolves against the parent's explicit height. If parent height is auto, the child's percentage height computes to auto too.

questions.question: Your mobile-first CSS uses min-width media queries. On a 375px phone, the desktop layout (intended for 1024px+) is showing. You've checked — the breakpoint is correct at 1024px. What's the most likely cause?

options.text: The <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width"> tag is missing. Without it, mobile browsers render at ~980px virtual width, triggering the 1024px query.
min-width media queries use physical pixels, not CSS pixels. A 375px phone at 3x density reports 1125 physical pixels, exceeding 1024px.
Mobile browsers always load the largest breakpoint first for performance, then scale down. Add a max-width query to override.
min-width queries are evaluated right-to-left in the cascade, so the last matching query wins regardless of specificity.

questions.explanation: Without the viewport meta tag, mobile browsers use a default ~980px layout viewport, making min-width: 1024px match on some devices.

questions.question: You have a CSS Grid with grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr). On narrow screens you want one column. You add a media query switching to 1fr but the three-column layout persists on mobile. Inspector shows your media query is loaded. What's wrong?

options.text: The original rule has higher specificity or appears later in the stylesheet. Your media query rule isn't overriding it — check selector specificity and source order.
CSS Grid ignores media queries. You need to switch to flexbox for responsive layouts because grid is fixed-column only.
repeat(3, 1fr) uses !important internally. Only adding !important to your media query can override it.
1fr in the media query creates one fractional unit of the three-column grid. You need to write grid-template-columns: 100% instead.

questions.explanation: Media queries don't boost specificity. If the original selector is more specific or appears later, it wins regardless of the media query.

questions.question: You animate an element's left property from 0 to 200px. The animation is visually janky at 60fps on a mid-range phone. DevTools shows layout thrashing during animation. What should you change?

options.text: Use transform: translateX(200px) instead. Transforms are compositor-only — they skip layout and paint, running on the GPU without triggering reflow.
Increase the animation duration. Janky animations are caused by too-short durations that overwhelm the browser's frame budget.
Wrap the element in a position: fixed container. Fixed elements are always GPU-accelerated regardless of the animated property.
Use requestAnimationFrame in JavaScript instead of CSS animations. JavaScript animations always run on the compositor thread.

questions.explanation: Animating left triggers layout on every frame. transform: translateX is handled by the compositor thread without layout/paint.

questions.question: You add transition: all 0.3s ease to a card component. Hovering works great, but now navigating between pages feels sluggish — every property animates when the component mounts with new data. What's the fix?

options.text: Replace 'all' with specific properties like transition: transform 0.3s ease, box-shadow 0.3s ease. 'all' transitions every CSS change including layout shifts on data updates.
Add transition-delay: 0 to force instant transitions on mount. The default delay causes the sluggish mounting behavior.
Use animation instead of transition. CSS animations don't fire on property changes, only on keyframe triggers.
Set will-change: all on the element. This pre-computes transitions so they're instant on mount.

questions.explanation: transition: all animates every property change, including unintended ones during re-renders. Always specify only the properties you want to animate.

questions.question: You build a tabbed interface using <div> elements with click handlers. Keyboard users report they can't navigate between tabs using Tab/Arrow keys. Mouse works fine. What's missing?

options.text: Divs aren't focusable by default. Add tabindex, role="tab", role="tablist" on the container, and keyboard event handlers for arrow key navigation.
CSS outline: none is suppressing keyboard focus. Remove it and keyboard navigation will work on any element.
JavaScript click handlers automatically create keyboard support in modern browsers. The issue is the browser — test in Chrome instead.
Add accesskey attributes to each tab div. This is the HTML standard for keyboard navigation of custom controls.

questions.explanation: <div> elements lack native keyboard interaction. Custom widgets need tabindex for focus, ARIA roles for semantics, and JS for arrow key handling.

questions.question: You have a sticky sidebar (position: sticky; top: 0) inside a flex container. It scrolls with the page instead of sticking. The parent flex container has overflow: auto for a different purpose. Why doesn't sticky work?

options.text: position: sticky is relative to its nearest scrolling ancestor. overflow: auto on the parent creates a new scroll container, so the sidebar sticks within that container, not the viewport.
Sticky positioning doesn't work inside flexbox. It's only compatible with block layout and CSS Grid.
The sidebar needs a fixed height for sticky to calculate its position. Without height, it falls back to relative positioning.
top: 0 conflicts with flexbox alignment. Sticky elements in flex containers require inset: 0 instead of top.

questions.explanation: Sticky positions relative to the nearest ancestor with overflow other than visible. overflow: auto creates a new scroll context, breaking viewport-relative stickiness.

questions.question: You use clamp(1rem, 2.5vw, 2rem) for fluid typography. On a 1920px monitor, font size is correct. On a 320px phone, text is unreadably small at about 8px. How do you fix the formula while keeping fluid scaling?

options.text: The minimum 1rem should protect against this, but 2.5vw at 320px = 8px which is less than the browser's computed 1rem only if root font-size was overridden. Check if html { font-size: 10px } exists — 1rem would then equal 10px, and 8px < 10px so clamp picks 1rem=10px which is still small.
clamp evaluates all three values as viewport units internally. The 1rem minimum is converted to vw before comparison, so it doesn't act as a true pixel floor.
Add !important to the clamp. Without it, the browser's default stylesheet overrides clamp with its own font-size algorithm on small screens.
Replace clamp with calc(1rem + 2.5vw). calc provides a true minimum because it adds to the base, whereas clamp picks the middle value which can be below 1rem.

questions.explanation: clamp's minimum only works if 1rem resolves to a readable size. If root font-size is set below 16px, the rem minimum is also small. Fix root font-size or adjust the min value.

questions.question: You animate opacity and transform on 50 list items simultaneously on page load. Despite using GPU-friendly properties, the animation stutters badly on first run but is smooth on subsequent triggers. What causes the initial stutter?

options.text: First animation triggers layer promotion for all 50 elements at once, causing a large composite layer tree creation spike. Use will-change on mount (before animation) to pre-promote layers, or stagger animations.
The browser caches animation keyframes after first run. Initial parse of 50 @keyframes blocks is expensive. Inline the styles instead.
GPU memory is cold on first render. The GPU needs a warm-up frame before it can handle parallel animations.
opacity and transform aren't actually GPU-accelerated on first paint. They only get compositor treatment after the initial layout pass.

questions.explanation: Promoting 50 elements to compositor layers simultaneously causes a one-time spike. will-change or staggered entry animations avoid the thundering-herd layer creation.

questions.question: You create buttons in a loop: for (var i = 0; i < 5; i++) { buttons[i].onclick = function() { alert(i); }; }. Every button alerts '5'. Why?

options.text: var is function-scoped, not block-scoped. All callbacks close over the same i variable. By the time any button is clicked, the loop is done and i is 5. Use let instead.
onclick handlers are deferred until the DOM finishes loading. By then, the loop variable has been garbage collected and defaults to the loop's upper bound.
alert() only accepts string literals, not variables. It coerces any variable to the last assigned value in the current scope.
The loop assigns the same function reference to all buttons. JavaScript reuses function objects, so they all share the final state.

questions.explanation: var has function scope — one i shared by all closures. After loop ends, i === 5. let creates a new binding per iteration.

questions.question: You write a counter: function makeCounter() { let count = 0; return { inc() { count++; }, get() { return count; } }; }. Two separate counters (a and b) created via makeCounter() — incrementing a doesn't affect b. Why?

options.text: Each call to makeCounter creates a new execution context with its own count variable. The returned methods close over their own context — completely independent closures.
let creates block-scoped variables that are automatically cloned when the function returns. Each return statement copies the variable.
The object literal { inc, get } creates a deep copy of all referenced variables. Objects in JavaScript always copy by value.
JavaScript detects naming collisions between a.count and b.count and automatically namespaces them under the hood.

questions.explanation: Each invocation creates a fresh scope with its own count. Closures capture their enclosing scope — not a shared global.

questions.question: You write: fetch('/api/data').then(res => res.json()); console.log(data). The console.log runs before the fetch completes and data is undefined. Why?

options.text: fetch is asynchronous — it returns a Promise and execution continues immediately. console.log runs before the Promise resolves. Move the log inside .then() or use await.
fetch only works inside async functions. In a regular function, it executes synchronously but returns null instead of the response.
The .then() callback runs on a separate thread. The main thread can't access variables set in other threads.
res.json() is synchronous but fetch() buffers the response. The data isn't available until the buffer flushes after the current event loop tick.

questions.explanation: Promises are async. Code after a .then() chain runs immediately without waiting. Use await or chain logic inside .then().

questions.question: You attach a click handler to a parent <ul>. Clicking a child <li> still triggers the parent's handler even though no handler is on the <li>. Why?

options.text: DOM events bubble up from the target element through ancestors. The click on <li> bubbles to <ul> where your handler catches it. This is called event delegation.
The browser automatically copies parent event handlers to all child elements during DOM construction for performance.
ul and li share an implicit event scope because they're list elements. Other element types don't exhibit this behavior.
Without stopPropagation on the <li>, the browser assumes the child wants to delegate to the parent by default.

questions.explanation: DOM events bubble from target to root by default. Event delegation exploits this by listening on a parent for child events.

questions.question: You have three independent API calls. You await each sequentially: await fetchA(); await fetchB(); await fetchC();. Each takes 1 second. Total time is ~3 seconds. How do you reduce this to ~1 second?

options.text: Use Promise.all([fetchA(), fetchB(), fetchC()]). All three start immediately and run concurrently. Total time equals the slowest call, not the sum.
Use Promise.race([fetchA(), fetchB(), fetchC()]). race() runs all calls simultaneously and returns all results when the fastest completes.
Wrap each in setTimeout(fn, 0) to move them to parallel threads. JavaScript timers create true multi-threaded execution.
Use Web Workers for each call. Async/await is single-threaded so concurrent network calls require separate worker threads.

questions.explanation: Promise.all fires all promises simultaneously and resolves when all complete. Sequential awaits unnecessarily serialize independent operations.

questions.question: You call [1,2,3].myMethod() and get 'myMethod is not a function'. You then add Array.prototype.myMethod = function() { ... } and it works. A colleague warns this is dangerous. Why?

options.text: Modifying built-in prototypes risks collisions — if a library or future JS version adds myMethod with different behavior, your code or theirs breaks silently. It also affects every array in the application.
Prototype methods run 10x slower than static functions because V8 can't optimize prototype chains beyond two levels.
Adding methods to Array.prototype triggers a global re-compilation of all JavaScript in the page, causing a noticeable performance freeze.
It's only dangerous in strict mode. In sloppy mode, prototype extensions are sandboxed to the current module.

questions.explanation: Extending native prototypes pollutes all instances globally and risks name collisions with future spec additions or libraries.

questions.question: You create a debounce function but notice it fires on every keystroke instead of waiting. Your code: function debounce(fn, ms) { setTimeout(() => fn(), ms); }. What's missing?

options.text: You're not clearing the previous timer. Each call creates a new setTimeout without canceling the prior one. Store the timer ID and call clearTimeout before setting a new one.
setTimeout doesn't work inside closures. Use requestAnimationFrame instead, which natively supports debouncing.
The delay parameter needs to be doubled because setTimeout measures in half-milliseconds internally.
You need to use setInterval instead of setTimeout. setTimeout fires once and can't restart, while setInterval resets automatically.

questions.explanation: Debouncing requires canceling the previous timer on each call. Without clearTimeout, every invocation creates an independent delayed call.

questions.question: You add a scroll listener to optimize a page: window.addEventListener('scroll', heavyCalculation). The page becomes extremely sluggish during scrolling. DevTools shows the handler fires hundreds of times per second. What's the fix?

options.text: Throttle the handler so it runs at most once per ~16ms (60fps). Scroll events fire on every pixel change — without throttling, expensive work blocks the main thread.
Use 'scrollend' event instead. It fires once when scrolling stops, avoiding repeated calls entirely.
Set the event listener to { passive: true }. Passive listeners run on a background thread and never block scrolling.
Replace addEventListener with onscroll property assignment. Property-based handlers have built-in browser throttling.

questions.explanation: Scroll fires rapidly. Throttling limits execution frequency. passive: true helps scroll performance but doesn't reduce how often your callback runs.

questions.question: You use Object.create(null) to create a config map. Later, config.hasOwnProperty('key') throws 'hasOwnProperty is not a function'. Objects always have this method — why not this one?

options.text: Object.create(null) creates an object with no prototype at all — no Object.prototype. Methods like hasOwnProperty live on Object.prototype and are inherited, so this object doesn't have them.
Object.create(null) creates a frozen object. Frozen objects disable method access to prevent mutation.
hasOwnProperty was removed in ES2020. Use Object.hasOwn() instead, which works on all objects.
null prototype objects only support symbol-keyed properties. String-keyed method calls are disabled for safety.

questions.explanation: Object.create(null) produces a truly empty object with no prototype chain. Use Object.hasOwn(obj, key) or Object.prototype.hasOwnProperty.call(obj, key).

questions.question: You wrap a third-party callback API: new Promise((resolve) => { thirdPartyCall(data, resolve); }). Sometimes the callback never fires and the promise hangs forever. async/await code after it never executes. What's the resilient pattern?

options.text: Race the promise against a timeout: Promise.race([yourPromise, new Promise((_, reject) => setTimeout(() => reject(new Error('timeout')), 5000))]). Ensures the await always resolves or rejects.
Add .finally() after the await. finally() always runs even if the promise never settles, acting as a fallback.
Use Promise.allSettled() with a single promise. allSettled returns immediately if the promise doesn't resolve within one event loop tick.
Set a global unhandledrejection listener. It fires when promises hang for more than 30 seconds by default.

questions.explanation: Promise.race with a timeout promise ensures you get either a result or an error. Promises that never settle will hang all downstream awaits without this.

questions.question: You add a touchstart listener to a button for instant response on mobile. But now every tap triggers both your touchstart handler AND the click handler 300ms later, causing a double action. What's happening and how do you fix it?

options.text: Mobile browsers fire touch events AND synthesize a click event for compatibility. Call event.preventDefault() in the touchstart handler to suppress the synthetic click, or use pointerdown which unifies both.
touchstart and click are the same event on mobile — the browser fires it twice due to a rendering bug. Add { once: true } to both listeners.
The 300ms delay is the browser's double-tap-to-zoom timer. Disable zoom via meta viewport and the duplicate click disappears.
Use stopImmediatePropagation() in the click handler. This prevents the touchstart event from re-entering the event queue.

questions.explanation: Mobile browsers synthesize click events after touch events for backward compatibility. preventDefault() on touch suppresses the synthetic click.

questions.question: You benchmark obj.x access on two objects: one created via { x: 1 } and another where x is set inside a constructor after other conditional properties. V8 DevTools show the second is significantly slower. Why?

options.text: V8 uses hidden classes (shapes). Objects with the same property order share a shape for fast access. Conditional properties create different shapes — the object becomes megamorphic, disabling inline caching.
Constructor-created objects are stored in a slower heap region. Object literals use the fast 'young generation' heap.
Conditional logic in constructors triggers V8's deoptimization flag, which forces interpretation mode for all property accesses.
The second object has more properties, and property access is O(n) in JavaScript. Fewer properties = faster lookup.

questions.explanation: V8 optimizes property access via hidden classes. Objects with inconsistent property initialization orders create polymorphic access sites, preventing inline cache hits.

questions.question: You define a function parameter as type string | number. Inside the function, you try to call .toFixed(2) directly and TypeScript errors. Why?

options.text: TypeScript can't guarantee the value is a number — it might be a string, which doesn't have toFixed. You need a type guard (typeof check) to narrow the union before calling number-specific methods.
Union types in TypeScript merge method signatures. toFixed should work because it exists on number. The error is a known TS compiler bug.
string | number creates an intersection, not a union. Intersections only expose methods common to both types.
toFixed is deprecated in TypeScript. Use Number.prototype.format() instead, which works on union types.

questions.explanation: On a union type, you can only access members common to all types. Narrow with typeof to access type-specific methods.

questions.question: You annotate a config object as { timeout: number; retries: number } but TypeScript allows you to assign { timeout: 5, retries: 3, debug: true } to it when passed inline to a function. When assigned to a variable first, it errors. Why the difference?

options.text: TypeScript performs excess property checking only on object literals directly assigned or passed inline. When assigned to a variable, the type widens and excess properties are allowed.

The question describes the opposite behavior. Object literals passed inline are the ones that DO trigger excess property errors. Variables with wider types are allowed because they might have extra properties.
TypeScript ignores extra properties in function arguments for backward compatibility. Variable assignment has stricter rules.
Inline objects are interpreted as 'any' type. Only explicit variable declarations enforce type shape.

questions.explanation: TypeScript's excess property checking catches extra properties on fresh object literals. Variables typed with a wider type are allowed by structural compatibility.

questions.question: You write function identity(arg: any): any { return arg; } and it works, but you lose type information — the return type is always any. A colleague suggests using generics. What's the improvement?

options.text: function identity<T>(arg: T): T — the generic T captures the input type and preserves it through to the return type. identity('hello') returns string, not any.
function identity(arg: unknown): typeof arg — unknown is the type-safe version of any and typeof automatically infers the return.
Overload the function with every possible type. Generics are just syntactic sugar for overloads.
Use 'as const' on the return: return arg as const. This locks the return type to the exact input type.

questions.explanation: Generics preserve type information through function calls. T captures the actual type at each call site, unlike any which discards it.

questions.question: You enable strict: true in tsconfig.json and suddenly get hundreds of errors in your existing codebase. Most are 'implicitly has an any type'. What does strict mode do here?

options.text: strict: true enables noImplicitAny (among other flags). Every parameter and variable that TypeScript can't infer a type for must be explicitly annotated — no more silent any fallback.
strict mode changes JavaScript runtime behavior, rejecting any values at execution time. Errors happen because the code worked in non-strict JS.
strict: true is deprecated and enables experimental type-checking rules that aren't stable yet.
It only affects .tsx files, not .ts. The errors come from JSX components lacking return type annotations.

questions.explanation: strict: true is an umbrella for strictNullChecks, noImplicitAny, strictFunctionTypes, etc. It catches implicit any types that silently pass otherwise.

questions.question: You write function getLength<T>(arg: T): number { return arg.length; } and TS errors that 'length does not exist on type T'. T could be anything. How do you constrain T to only types with a length?

options.text: Use a generic constraint: <T extends { length: number }>. This restricts T to types that have a numeric length property. Arrays and strings qualify; numbers don't.
Cast arg to any inside the function: (arg as any).length. Constraints are unnecessary when you know the runtime behavior.
Use T extends Array<unknown>. Only arrays have length; strings use .size() in TypeScript.
Add a runtime check: if ('length' in arg). TypeScript automatically narrows T when you use 'in' operator, no constraint needed.

questions.explanation: Generic constraints via extends ensure T must have the required shape. TS then knows arg.length exists without unsafe casts.

questions.question: You have an interface User { name: string; email: string; age: number; }. You need a function that accepts an object to update just some fields (partial update). How do you type the parameter?

options.text: Use Partial<User>. It makes all properties optional: { name?: string; email?: string; age?: number; }. The caller can pass any subset of fields.
Use Pick<User, 'name' | 'email' | 'age'>. Pick makes all selected properties optional by default.
Use Omit<User, never>. Omitting nothing from User makes all fields optional.
Type it as User | undefined. This allows either a full User object or nothing, covering partial updates.

questions.explanation: Partial<T> makes every property optional. It's the standard utility type for partial/patch update patterns.

questions.question: You have a function that returns null for 'not found'. With strictNullChecks enabled, callers must handle null even in contexts where you know the item exists. How do you tell TypeScript 'I'm certain this isn't null' at a specific call site?

options.text: Use the non-null assertion operator: result!.property. The ! postfix tells TypeScript you guarantee this value is not null/undefined at this point.
Cast to any: (result as any).property. any bypasses null checks and all other type checking.
Disable strictNullChecks for that file with a directive comment. Per-file configuration overrides tsconfig.
Wrap in try/catch. TypeScript narrows types inside catch blocks, removing null from the type.

questions.explanation: The ! non-null assertion operator removes null/undefined from the type. Use sparingly — it bypasses safety, so prefer runtime checks when possible.

questions.question: You want a type that is User but without the password field — for API responses. You try Omit<User, 'password'> but notice Omit doesn't error if you misspell the key as 'passwrod'. How can you make key omission type-safe?

options.text: Create a strict omit: type StrictOmit<T, K extends keyof T> = Omit<T, K>. Constraining K to keyof T ensures only valid keys are accepted.
Use Pick to select the fields you want instead. Pick<User, 'name' | 'email'> is inherently type-safe since non-existent keys error.
Omit always validates keys in TypeScript 5+. You must be using an older version.
Use the built-in Exclude<User, 'password'> instead. Exclude is the type-safe version of Omit.

questions.explanation: Built-in Omit accepts any string for keys. Constraining K extends keyof T catches typos at compile time. Alternatively, Pick is safe by default.

questions.question: You write a generic function merge<T, U>(a: T, b: U): T & U. When you pass objects with overlapping keys of different types, TypeScript compiles fine but runtime values are wrong — the second object's values overwrite the first. Is this a TS bug?

options.text: No. T & U (intersection) means a value must satisfy both types. TypeScript assumes overlapping keys are compatible. At runtime, Object.assign or spread overwrites — the type doesn't prevent this.
Yes. Intersection types should error on overlapping keys with different types. File a TypeScript issue.
No. The runtime is correct. Intersection types mean the later type's values take priority — the type system reflects this.
No. Use extends instead of &. extends checks for key conflicts at compile time.

questions.explanation: Intersection types assume compatibility. When runtime merging overwrites conflicting keys, the type assertion becomes inaccurate. Add constraints or use branded types.

questions.question: You import a CommonJS library in your ESM TypeScript project. It works with esModuleInterop: true but fails without it — you get 'default is not a function'. What does esModuleInterop actually fix?

options.text: CommonJS modules export via module.exports (no default export). Without esModuleInterop, import lib from 'lib' maps to lib.default which doesn't exist. esModuleInterop adds a helper that maps module.exports to default.
esModuleInterop changes the Node.js module resolution algorithm. Without it, TypeScript can't locate .cjs files.
It enables tree-shaking for CommonJS modules. Without it, the entire module loads and 'default' refers to the un-shaken bundle.
It polyfills ES module syntax in the output. Without it, import/export statements aren't transpiled for CommonJS targets.

questions.explanation: CJS has no default export concept. esModuleInterop emits __importDefault helper that wraps module.exports as { default: module.exports }, enabling import X from 'cjs-lib'.

questions.question: You build a form library where field validation rules should mirror the form data shape. For { name: string; age: number }, validators should be { name: (v: string) => Error | null; age: (v: number) => Error | null }. How do you derive this type automatically?

options.text: Use a mapped type: type Validators<T> = { [K in keyof T]: (value: T[K]) => Error | null }. This iterates over T's keys, using each key's value type as the validator's parameter type.
Use Record<keyof T, (value: unknown) => Error | null>. Record distributes the validator function across all keys. Use type assertion inside each validator.
Create a generic Validator<V> type and manually intersect: Validators = Validator<string> & Validator<number>. Mapped types can't access per-key value types.
Use conditional types: type Validators<T> = T extends infer U ? { [K in keyof U]: Function } : never. Conditional inference extracts each field type.

questions.explanation: Mapped types { [K in keyof T]: ... } iterate over each key, and T[K] accesses the corresponding value type — perfect for deriving parallel type structures.

questions.question: Your monorepo has packages A and B. Package A imports from B. TypeScript compiles fine but at runtime you get 'module not found' for B's imports. Both packages have their own tsconfig. What's likely misconfigured?

options.text: TypeScript project references are missing. Without references and composite: true in B's tsconfig, TS resolves B's source files for type checking but the compiled output paths don't match the import paths at runtime.
TypeScript can't cross package boundaries. You need to publish B to npm even for local development.
The baseUrl in A's tsconfig needs to point to the monorepo root. TypeScript path resolution is always relative to baseUrl.
TypeScript only resolves .ts files. Package B needs to export .ts files directly instead of compiled .js.

questions.explanation: Project references ensure TS builds B first and that runtime paths match. Without composite/references, TS resolves source but output structure mismatches cause runtime failures.

questions.question: You call useState inside an if statement: if (loggedIn) { const [user, setUser] = useState(null); }. React throws 'Rendered more hooks than during the previous render'. Why?

options.text: React tracks hooks by call order, not by name. Conditional hooks change the order between renders — hook 1 might be useState on one render and useEffect on the next. Hooks must be called unconditionally at the top level.
useState can't accept null as initial value. The error is about the null argument, not the conditional. Use undefined instead.
if statements create a new React scope. Hooks in nested scopes are treated as hooks from a child component.
useState must be called before any if statements in the component. It works if you move the if statement lower in the function.

questions.explanation: React relies on hook call order being identical across renders. Conditional or loop hooks break this invariant. Always call hooks at the top level.

questions.question: Your component re-renders every time the parent re-renders, even though its props haven't changed. The component is a large data table and re-renders are slow. What's the simplest optimization?

options.text: Wrap the component with React.memo(). It performs a shallow comparison of props and skips re-render if they haven't changed.
Move the component to a separate file. React only re-renders components defined in the same file as the parent.
Add key={Math.random()} to the component. Random keys tell React the component is unchanged.
Use useRef instead of useState in the parent. useRef never causes child re-renders.

questions.explanation: React.memo memoizes the component, skipping re-render when props are shallowly equal. Default behavior is to re-render on every parent render.

questions.question: You call setCount(count + 1) three times in a row in a click handler, expecting count to increase by 3. It only increases by 1. Why?

options.text: All three calls use the same stale count value from the current render. React batches state updates. Use the functional form: setCount(prev => prev + 1) to chain off the pending state.
React rate-limits setState to one call per event handler. Additional calls are silently ignored for performance.
setCount is asynchronous. The second and third calls execute before the first one resolves, causing a race condition.
State updates are synchronous in event handlers. The issue is that count + 1 overflows when called rapidly.

questions.explanation: State updates in a single handler use the same snapshot. Functional updates (prev => prev + 1) chain correctly because each receives the latest pending state.

questions.question: You render a list of items using items.map(item => <Item ... />) and React warns 'Each child in a list should have a unique key prop'. You add key={index}. A colleague says index keys are problematic. Why?

options.text: When items are reordered, added, or removed, index-based keys associate the wrong state with the wrong item. React reuses component instances by key — index keys cause stale state bugs.
Index keys are slower than string keys. React hashes string keys for O(1) diffing but iterates sequentially for numeric keys.
Index keys only work in development mode. Production builds strip numeric keys for bundle size.
Index keys cause memory leaks. React caches components by numeric key indefinitely, while string keys are garbage collected.

questions.explanation: Index keys break when list order changes. React matches old and new items by key — wrong key = wrong component instance/state. Use stable unique IDs.

questions.question: Your useEffect fetches data on mount but the effect fires twice in development, causing duplicate API calls. The component isn't unmounting/remounting in your code. What's happening?

options.text: React 18 Strict Mode intentionally double-invokes effects in development to help find missing cleanup functions. Add a cleanup that aborts the fetch, or accept this as dev-only behavior.
useEffect with an empty dependency array always fires twice — once on mount, once after the first paint. This is by design for performance.
Your bundler's hot module replacement re-mounts the component. Disable HMR to fix it.
The React DevTools extension injects a second render cycle. Disable the extension and the duplicate goes away.

questions.explanation: React 18 Strict Mode remounts components in dev to expose effects that don't clean up. Add an AbortController cleanup. This doesn't happen in production.

questions.question: You pass an inline object as a prop: <Child config={{ theme: 'dark' }} />. Even with React.memo on Child, it re-renders every time. Why?

options.text: Inline objects create a new reference on every render. React.memo does shallow comparison — new reference !== old reference. Memoize with useMemo: const config = useMemo(() => ({ theme: 'dark' }), []).
React.memo doesn't work with object props, only primitives. Use useCallback for object props.
The child component needs to also use React.memo internally for nested props to be compared.
Inline objects are always treated as 'changed' by React's reconciler regardless of memo. Move the object to state instead.

questions.explanation: Every render creates a new object literal with a new reference. Shallow comparison sees different references. useMemo stabilizes the reference.

questions.question: You use useEffect to subscribe to a WebSocket. After navigating away and back, you see duplicate messages. The effect's cleanup function calls ws.close(). What's likely wrong?

options.text: The cleanup function creates a new closure each render but closes the wrong WebSocket instance. Store the WebSocket in a useRef so cleanup always references the current connection.
WebSocket.close() is asynchronous. The component unmounts before close completes, leaving the old connection alive. Use synchronous disconnect.
The dependency array includes a value that changes on navigation, causing the effect to re-run without proper cleanup sequencing.
React.StrictMode prevents cleanup functions from running. Disable it to fix WebSocket cleanup.

questions.explanation: If a dependency changes on navigation, the effect re-subscribes. If cleanup doesn't properly close the old connection (e.g., stale reference), duplicates occur. Audit the dependency array.

questions.question: You have a deeply nested state object and update a property: setState({ ...state, user: { ...state.user, address: { ...state.user.address, city: 'NYC' } } }). This is becoming unmanageable. What's the cleanest approach?

options.text: Use useReducer with Immer's produce function, or use a library like zustand with Immer. Immer lets you write mutable-style code that produces immutable updates: state.user.address.city = 'NYC'.
Mutate state directly: state.user.address.city = 'NYC'; setState(state). React detects deep changes automatically.
Flatten the state into separate useState calls for each leaf value. Never use nested objects in state.
Use JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(state)) to deep clone, modify the clone, then setState. This is the standard React pattern.

questions.explanation: Immer enables mutable syntax with immutable results. useReducer + Immer handles complex nested state cleanly without manual spreading.

questions.question: You build a <Modal> component that renders a dialog. It's visually clipped by a parent with overflow: hidden. The modal needs to appear above everything. What's the React-idiomatic solution?

options.text: Use ReactDOM.createPortal to render the modal into document.body (or a dedicated div). Portals render children outside the parent DOM hierarchy while preserving React context and event bubbling.
Set z-index: 999999 on the modal. z-index overrides overflow: hidden at any nesting level.
Use position: fixed on the modal. Fixed elements are always rendered above overflow: hidden parents.
Move the modal component to the top of the React tree. Components must be DOM-adjacent to their visual position.

questions.explanation: Portals escape the parent DOM tree, avoiding overflow/z-index stacking context issues. Events still bubble through the React tree, not the DOM tree.

questions.question: You notice a form with 20 input fields re-renders all fields on every keystroke in any single input. Each field is its own component. React Profiler shows the parent re-renders, triggering all children. State is managed in the parent. How do you fix this without changing architecture?

options.text: React.memo each field component and stabilize callback props with useCallback. The parent re-renders on every keystroke (state change), but memoized children skip re-render when their specific props haven't changed.
Use useRef for form state instead of useState. Refs don't trigger re-renders, so typing won't cause any component updates.
Wrap all inputs in React.Fragment. Fragments batch child updates and prevent individual re-renders.
Add shouldComponentUpdate returning false to the parent. This prevents re-renders from propagating to children.

questions.explanation: React.memo + useCallback ensures each field only re-renders when its own value or handlers change. Parent re-rendering won't cascade to memoized children with stable props.

questions.question: You build a <DataTable> component that needs to be extended with sorting, filtering, pagination, and row selection — each independently optional. Inheritance won't work. What pattern scales best?

options.text: Composition with headless hooks: useSorting(), useFiltering(), usePagination(), useSelection(). Each hook manages its own logic, returning state and handlers. The table component consumes only the hooks it needs.
Higher-order components: withSorting(withFiltering(withPagination(DataTable))). Each HOC wraps the table and injects props.
Render props for each feature. Pass four render prop functions that each control a different behavior.
Context providers for each feature. Wrap <DataTable> in <SortProvider><FilterProvider><PaginationProvider>... for composability.

questions.explanation: Headless hooks provide composable logic without wrapper hell. Each hook is independently testable and type-safe. HOCs and nested render props create hard-to-debug wrapper stacks.

questions.question: You use React.lazy() with Suspense for code splitting. A lazy-loaded component fails to load (network error). The entire page shows the fallback forever. How do you handle load failures gracefully?

options.text: Wrap the Suspense boundary with an Error Boundary. React.lazy rejects on network failure, and Error Boundaries catch render-time errors, letting you show a retry UI instead of an infinite fallback.
Add an onError prop to Suspense: <Suspense onError={handleError}>. Suspense has built-in error handling.
Use try/catch around the lazy import. Errors in lazy() are caught by standard JavaScript error handling.
Set a timeout on Suspense: <Suspense fallback={...} timeout={5000}>. After timeout, Suspense gives up and renders the children anyway.

questions.explanation: Suspense handles loading states; Error Boundaries handle failures. They're complementary — wrap Suspense with an Error Boundary that offers a retry button.

questions.question: Your SPA takes 8 seconds to load on 3G. DevTools shows a single 2MB JavaScript bundle. The user sees a white screen until it loads. What's the highest-impact fix?

options.text: Code split the bundle with dynamic imports. Route-based splitting loads only the code for the current page. Reduces initial bundle from 2MB to ~200KB, dramatically cutting load time.
Minify the JavaScript. Minification typically removes 80%+ of bundle size, bringing 2MB to under 400KB.
Move the script tag to the bottom of <body>. This ensures HTML renders before JavaScript blocks the page.
Add a loading spinner in the HTML. This doesn't reduce load time but eliminates the perceived white screen.

questions.explanation: Code splitting via dynamic import() is the highest impact — you only load what's needed. Minification helps but rarely achieves 80% reduction alone.

questions.question: Your page loads a 500KB CSS file for a component library, but this page only uses a modal and a button from it. First Contentful Paint is slow. What's the issue?

options.text: CSS is render-blocking — the browser can't paint until the entire 500KB stylesheet is parsed. Tree-shake unused CSS with PurgeCSS or only import the specific component styles you need.
500KB of CSS is within normal limits. The slow FCP is caused by server response time, not the stylesheet size.
CSS files are only render-blocking if they contain @import statements. A single flat CSS file loads asynchronously.
The browser caches CSS after first visit, so this only affects first-time visitors. Ignore it.

questions.explanation: CSS blocks rendering. Every unused kilobyte delays First Contentful Paint. Import only what you use or purge unused styles.

questions.question: Your blog page loads 30 images below the fold on initial page load. Lighthouse flags 'Offscreen images' as a major issue. What does this mean?

options.text: Images not visible in the viewport are downloaded immediately anyway, wasting bandwidth and competing with critical resources. Add loading="lazy" to offscreen images so they load only when scrolled into view.
Offscreen images have incorrect dimensions. Lighthouse wants explicit width and height to prevent layout shift.
Images below the fold need alt text. Lighthouse flags missing accessibility attributes as 'offscreen images'.
The images are positioned off-screen with CSS (negative margins). Lighthouse detects hidden images that waste bandwidth.

questions.explanation: Offscreen images consume bandwidth and delay the critical rendering path. loading="lazy" defers them until the user scrolls near them.

questions.question: Users report the site loads slowly on every visit. Your server returns Cache-Control: no-store for all assets including CSS and JS files. Why is this a problem?

options.text: no-store prevents the browser from caching anything. Every visit re-downloads all assets. Use Cache-Control: max-age=31536000, immutable for hashed static assets.
no-store only affects images, not CSS and JS. The slowness is caused by something else.
no-store is correct for security. Caching CSS and JS creates XSS vulnerabilities because attackers can modify cached files.
Browsers ignore Cache-Control for local assets. The issue is the CDN not caching, not the browser.

questions.explanation: no-store forces full re-download every time. Static assets with content hashes in filenames should use long max-age since the hash changes when content changes.

questions.question: Users report a visible 'flash' where the page layout shifts after images load. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) score is 0.4 (poor). Images don't have dimensions. What's the connection?

options.text: Without explicit width and height attributes, the browser allocates zero space for images initially. When images load, content shifts to accommodate them. Always set dimensions or use aspect-ratio in CSS to reserve space.
CLS measures how long it takes for the layout to stabilize, not visual shifts. Slow image loading increases the time metric.
The flash is caused by FOUC (Flash of Unstyled Content), not layout shift. Add a CSS preload link to fix it.
CLS only applies to text content. Image loading doesn't affect layout shift calculations.

questions.explanation: CLS measures unexpected layout shifts. Images without dimensions cause shifts when they load and push content around. Specify width/height or use aspect-ratio.

questions.question: You add a font from Google Fonts via <link>. Users on slow connections see a flash of invisible text (FOIT) for 3 seconds before the font appears. How do you fix the invisible text?

options.text: Add font-display: swap to the @font-face rule (or &display=swap in the Google Fonts URL). This shows a fallback system font immediately, swapping to the custom font when loaded.
Preload the font: <link rel="preload" as="font">. This guarantees the font loads before any text renders.
Convert the font to a data URI in CSS. Inline fonts always load synchronously with the stylesheet.
Use JavaScript to detect font loading and show text only after the font is ready. This eliminates the flash entirely.

questions.explanation: font-display: swap tells the browser to use a fallback font immediately, then swap when the custom font loads. No invisible text period.

questions.question: You deploy a CSS fix and users still see the old styles. The CSS file has Cache-Control: max-age=31536000 (1 year). How do you force cache invalidation without changing headers?

options.text: Use content-hashed filenames: styles.a3b4c5.css. When content changes, the hash changes, creating a new URL that bypasses the cache. The HTML references the new filename.
Add a query string: styles.css?v=2. All browsers treat query strings as cache-busters that force fresh downloads.
Set meta http-equiv="cache-control" in HTML. Meta tags override server cache headers.
Ask users to hard-refresh (Ctrl+Shift+R). This is the standard cache invalidation approach for production sites.

questions.explanation: Content hashing (styles.[hash].css) is the reliable cache-busting strategy. New content = new hash = new URL = cache miss. Query strings are unreliable with some CDNs.

questions.question: You serve the same 1200px wide hero image to all devices. On a 375px mobile screen, the image downloads at full size. What's the correct approach to serve appropriately sized images?

options.text: Use srcset and sizes attributes: . The browser picks the best size based on viewport and pixel density.
Use CSS max-width: 100% on the image. This resizes the image visually, so the browser only downloads the visible portion.
Detect screen width with JavaScript and dynamically change the src attribute. This is the only way to serve different images.
Serve WebP format. WebP automatically adapts resolution based on the requesting device.

questions.explanation: srcset with width descriptors lets the browser choose the optimal image size. CSS only affects display, not download. JS detection misses the browser's preload scanner.

questions.question: You add <link rel="preload" as="script" href="analytics.js"> expecting faster page load. Instead, Lighthouse warns about preloaded resources not being used within 3 seconds. The script loads in a footer component rendered later. What's wrong?

options.text: Preload downloads resources at high priority immediately but expects them to be used quickly. For resources needed later, use <link rel="prefetch"> which downloads at low priority for future navigation or use.
Preload only works for CSS and fonts, not JavaScript. Use defer or async attributes instead.
The as="script" attribute is wrong. Preloaded scripts need as="fetch" to avoid the timing warning.
Preload requires a crossorigin attribute for same-origin scripts. Without it, the browser downloads it but can't use it.

questions.explanation: Preload is for critical resources needed within seconds. For non-critical future resources, prefetch downloads at low priority without competing with critical requests.

questions.question: You have a chat app where new messages are appended to a scrollable container. After 1000+ messages, adding a new message causes a 200ms jank. React Profiler shows the list component takes 180ms to re-render. What's the best solution?

options.text: Use virtualization (react-window or react-virtuoso). Only render the ~20 visible messages in the DOM. As the user scrolls, swap elements in and out. 1000 items in state but only ~20 DOM nodes.
Use React.memo on each message component. This prevents re-rendering unchanged messages when a new one is added.
Switch from useState to useRef for the messages array. This prevents any re-renders when messages change.
Use web workers to render the message list off the main thread. Workers can handle DOM updates without blocking.

questions.explanation: Virtualization drastically reduces DOM nodes. React.memo helps but 1000 DOM elements still cause layout/paint costs. Virtualization solves the root cause.

questions.question: Your app uses a service worker to cache API responses. Users report seeing stale data after you push a backend update. The service worker returns cached responses without revalidating. What caching strategy should you switch to?

options.text: Stale-while-revalidate: return the cached response immediately for speed, but fetch the latest from the network in the background and update the cache. Next request gets fresh data.
Cache-first with no expiry is correct. The issue is the backend should send cache-bust headers that service workers respect.
Network-only strategy. Service workers shouldn't cache API data — only static assets.
Delete the service worker on every deploy. Register a new one to flush all cached API responses.

questions.explanation: Stale-while-revalidate balances speed (instant cached response) with freshness (background update). Cache-first without revalidation causes the reported staleness.

questions.question: Your e-commerce site has a product page with 50 thumbnail images. You've already added loading="lazy" but Lighthouse still flags LCP (Largest Contentful Paint) as slow. The main product image IS the LCP element. What's wrong?

options.text: loading="lazy" should NOT be on the LCP image. Lazy loading delays the most important image. Use fetchpriority="high" and eager loading on the LCP image. Only lazy-load below-fold thumbnails.
Lazy loading only works on tags. If the LCP element is a CSS background-image, lazy loading doesn't apply.
LCP measures server response time, not image loading. The slow LCP is a backend issue, not a frontend one.
Add preload for all 50 thumbnails. Preloading ensures they don't compete with the main image.

questions.explanation: Never lazy-load the LCP element — it delays the most critical content. Use fetchpriority="high" to boost it and lazy-load only non-critical images.

questions.question: You build a custom dropdown using <div> elements. Screen reader users report they can't tell it's a dropdown or navigate options. Visually it looks perfect. What's missing?

options.text: Add ARIA roles: role="listbox" on the container, role="option" on each item, aria-expanded on the trigger, and aria-activedescendant for the current selection. Native semantics don't exist on divs.
Add title attributes to each div. Screen readers read title attributes as semantic hints for custom elements.
Use a higher z-index so the dropdown appears above other content. Screen readers navigate based on visual layering.
Add tabindex="-1" to all items. Negative tabindex makes elements focusable, and screen readers announce focusable elements as interactive.

questions.explanation: Custom widgets built from divs need ARIA roles, states, and properties to expose semantics. Without them, screen readers see a flat series of text.

questions.question: You notice your site has outline: none on all focusable elements (a global CSS reset). A keyboard user reports they can't tell which element is focused. What should you do?

options.text: Remove outline: none or replace it with a custom :focus-visible style. Focus indicators are essential for keyboard users. :focus-visible only shows the outline for keyboard navigation, not mouse clicks.
Add a tooltip that appears on focus showing 'Currently focused'. This provides a visual indicator without an outline.
Use JavaScript to detect keyboard users and add outlines only for them. Mouse users shouldn't see focus outlines.
Change the cursor to a pointer on :focus. Keyboard users can follow the cursor position to know which element is focused.

questions.explanation: :focus-visible applies focus styles only when focus comes from keyboard (not mouse). It replaces the old pattern of removing outlines entirely.

questions.question: Your page has a decorative background image of abstract shapes in an tag. A screen reader announces 'image abstract-bg-pattern.png' which confuses users. How do you fix this?

options.text: Add an empty alt attribute: alt="". This tells screen readers the image is decorative and should be skipped. Without any alt, screen readers fall back to announcing the filename.
Add aria-hidden="true" on the image. This removes it from the accessibility tree. Both approaches work, but alt="" is the HTML standard for decorative images.
Use a CSS background-image instead of . CSS backgrounds are always ignored by screen readers and don't need alt text.
Add alt="decorative image". Describing the image as decorative helps screen reader users understand its purpose.

questions.explanation: alt="" signals a decorative image — screen readers skip it. Missing alt causes filename announcement. alt="decorative image" adds unnecessary noise.

questions.question: Your error message only uses red text color to indicate an error — no icon, no text change, just red. What accessibility problem does this create?

options.text: Color-blind users (8% of men) can't distinguish red text from normal text. Information must not be conveyed by color alone — add an error icon, bold text, or a text prefix like 'Error:'.
Red text has lower contrast than black text. The issue is purely a contrast ratio problem — use a darker red.
Screen readers can't detect text color changes. Add aria-label="error" to the text element.
Red is culturally inappropriate in some regions where it means 'success'. Use universal colors instead.

questions.explanation: WCAG 1.4.1: Don't use color as the sole means of conveying information. Supplement with icons, text, patterns, or other visual cues.

questions.question: You add aria-label="Close" to a <button> that already has visible text 'X'. A screen reader user reports hearing 'Close button' instead of 'X button'. They want to hear both. What's happening?

options.text: aria-label overrides the element's visible text content entirely. Screen readers use aria-label when present and ignore the inner text. To expose both, remove aria-label and use aria-describedby for supplemental text.
aria-label is appended to visible text. The user is hearing 'Close' because the screen reader clips 'X' as a single character. The behavior is correct.
aria-label only works on non-interactive elements. On buttons, it's ignored in favor of textContent.
Screen readers prioritize aria-label alphabetically. Since 'C' comes before 'X', 'Close' is announced first.

questions.explanation: aria-label fully overrides the accessible name from text content. If you want supplemental info, use aria-describedby. aria-label is not additive.

questions.question: A modal dialog opens on button click, but keyboard focus stays on the button behind the modal. Users can Tab to elements behind the opaque overlay. What's the correct fix?

options.text: Implement a focus trap: move focus into the modal on open, cycle Tab within modal elements only, and return focus to the trigger button on close. This is required by WAI-ARIA dialog pattern.
Add tabindex="-1" to all elements outside the modal. This removes them from the tab order while the modal is open.
Use pointer-events: none on the background. This prevents mouse and keyboard interaction with elements behind the modal.
Set aria-hidden="true" on the modal. This tells assistive technology to focus on the background content instead.

questions.explanation: Modal dialogs must trap focus — Tab cycles within the modal only. On close, focus returns to the element that opened it. This is the WAI-ARIA authoring pattern.

questions.question: You have a live chat widget where new messages appear dynamically. Screen reader users don't notice new messages unless they manually navigate to the chat area. How do you announce updates automatically?

options.text: Add aria-live="polite" to the chat messages container. Screen readers will announce new content added to a live region without interrupting the user's current activity.
Use JavaScript to call window.speechSynthesis.speak() when new messages arrive. This is the standard API for screen reader announcements.
Focus the chat container programmatically on each new message. Screen readers announce whatever receives focus.
Add role="alert" to each message element. Alerts are always announced immediately and are the correct role for chat messages.

questions.explanation: aria-live="polite" announces content changes at the next pause in speech. role="alert" is for urgent warnings and would be too disruptive for chat messages.

questions.question: Your design uses light gray text (#999) on a white background (#fff) for secondary text. Lighthouse reports a contrast ratio of 2.85:1. WCAG AA requires 4.5:1. The designer insists on the light gray aesthetic. What's the compromise?

options.text: Darken the gray to at least #767676 (4.54:1 ratio) which is the lightest gray that passes AA on white. The visual difference is subtle but the accessibility impact is significant.
Increase the font size to 24px. Large text only requires 3:1 ratio, so #999 would pass.
Add a text-shadow to increase perceived contrast. Text shadows are counted in WCAG contrast calculations.
Add an aria-label with the text content. Screen readers bypass contrast requirements when aria-label is present.

questions.explanation: #767676 on white = 4.54:1, the minimum for AA normal text. B works only if all secondary text is genuinely 18pt+ (which is unlikely).

questions.question: You use aria-hidden="true" on a parent container to hide decorative content from screen readers. Inside that container, there's a focusable button that keyboard users can still Tab to. What problem does this create?

options.text: The button is focusable (in the tab order) but invisible to screen readers (aria-hidden). This creates a ghost element — keyboard users reach it but hear nothing announced. Remove focusability or remove aria-hidden.
aria-hidden only hides text content, not interactive elements. The button still works correctly for both keyboard and screen reader users.
Nested focusable elements override aria-hidden on parents. The screen reader will still announce the button.
This only affects JAWS. NVDA and VoiceOver correctly handle focusable elements inside aria-hidden containers.

questions.explanation: Focusable elements inside aria-hidden create an accessibility trap. Users Tab to an element that's invisible to assistive tech. Either inert the container or make the button unfocusable.

questions.question: Your single-page app uses client-side routing. After navigation, keyboard focus stays on the link that was clicked, and screen readers don't announce the new page content. What should happen on route change?

options.text: Move focus to the new page's main heading or a skip-navigation target. Announce the new page via document.title update and an aria-live region. Without this, screen reader users don't know the page changed.
Add role="navigation" to the router component. This tells screen readers that content below it changes dynamically.
Call location.reload() on route change. Full page loads automatically handle focus and announcements.
Use React.Fragment for all route components. Fragments are transparent to screen readers and handle focus automatically.

questions.explanation: SPA route changes don't trigger the browser's native page-load behavior. You must manually manage focus and announce page changes for screen reader users.

questions.question: You build a data visualization (bar chart) using <canvas>. Screen readers announce 'image' or nothing at all. Sighted users understand the data instantly, but blind users get zero information. What's the accessible approach?

options.text: Add role="img" with a descriptive aria-label for the overall chart, and provide an accessible alternative like a hidden data table within the canvas fallback content, linked via aria-describedby.
Use SVG instead of canvas. SVGs are automatically accessible because each element can have its own ARIA attributes and text content.
Add alt text to the canvas tag. Canvas supports alt like img does.
Generate an audio representation of the data using Web Audio API. Screen reader users prefer sonification over text descriptions.

questions.explanation: Canvas is opaque to screen readers. Provide text alternatives: role="img" + aria-label for summary, plus a hidden data table for detail. SVG can also help but needs manual ARIA.

questions.question: You implement dark mode. Your contrast ratios all pass in light mode, but in dark mode, blue links (#3b82f6) on the dark background (#1a1a2e) have a 3.8:1 ratio — failing AA. You can't change the background. How do you fix it while keeping links visually distinct?

options.text: Lighten the blue to something like #60a5fa (5.2:1 ratio on dark) AND add an underline. Two visual indicators (color + underline) distinguish links, and the lighter blue meets contrast requirements.
Add font-weight: bold to links. Bold text has a 3:1 requirement instead of 4.5:1, so 3.8:1 passes.
Add a text-shadow glow effect. The glow increases perceived contrast beyond the calculated ratio.
Dark mode is not subject to WCAG requirements because it's an optional user preference.

questions.explanation: Adjust the color to meet 4.5:1 ratio on the dark background. Underlines help distinguish links from surrounding text, satisfying WCAG 1.4.1 (not by color alone).

questions.question: You write a unit test for a function that formats currency. The test calls the function and checks the return value. A colleague asks why you don't test that it updates the DOM. What's the right response?

options.text: Unit tests verify a single function/module in isolation. Testing DOM updates is an integration or component test. The formatter's job is to return a formatted string — test that contract only.
DOM testing is impossible in unit test frameworks like Jest. You need Selenium or Playwright for any DOM assertion.
Unit tests should test everything the function touches, including DOM side effects. Your test is incomplete.
DOM assertions make tests faster because they skip function call overhead. You should test the DOM instead of the return value.

questions.explanation: Unit tests verify isolated behavior. A pure formatting function should be tested by input/output only. DOM integration is a separate testing concern.

questions.question: Your test passes when run alone but fails when run with the full test suite. The test relies on a global variable set by a previous test. What's the problem?

options.text: Tests share global state, creating implicit dependencies on execution order. Each test must set up and tear down its own state. Use beforeEach to reset globals so tests are independent.
Jest runs tests in random order by default. Add --runInBand to force sequential execution matching the file order.
Global variables are frozen during test execution. A previous test's modification is cached and bleeds into yours.
The test file needs 'use strict' to isolate its scope from other test files.

questions.explanation: Tests must be independent and not rely on side effects from other tests. Use beforeEach/afterEach to set up clean state for each test.

questions.question: You test a component that fetches data from an API. Tests are slow and flaky because they make real HTTP requests. Sometimes the API is down. What's the standard fix?

options.text: Mock the fetch/API layer. Use jest.mock() or MSW (Mock Service Worker) to intercept requests and return predetermined responses. Tests become fast, deterministic, and independent of the API.
Add retry logic to the test. Flaky tests are normal with API dependencies — retrying 3 times makes them reliable.
Run a local copy of the API in a Docker container. This ensures the API is always available.
Skip the API tests in CI and only run them locally. CI doesn't have network access.

questions.explanation: Mock external dependencies in unit/component tests. Real API calls belong in integration/E2E tests. MSW intercepts at the network level for realistic mocking.

questions.question: Your unit tests for a UserProfile component all pass, but when rendered inside the actual app layout, the profile data doesn't appear. The component depends on a context provider that unit tests mocked out. What level of testing catches this?

options.text: Integration testing. Render the component with its real context providers, router, and parent components. This catches wiring issues between components that unit tests with mocks miss.
More thorough unit tests. Add test cases that verify every prop and context combination.
Visual regression testing. Screenshot comparisons would catch the missing profile data.
Linting. ESLint rules can detect when required context providers are missing from the component tree.

questions.explanation: Integration tests verify that components work together correctly. Unit tests with mocks verify isolated behavior but miss wiring issues between real dependencies.

questions.question: You mock Date.now() in a test to return a fixed timestamp. The test passes, but now other tests in the same file that depend on real time are broken. What's the correct practice?

options.text: Restore the original Date.now in afterEach: jest.restoreAllMocks() or save and restore the original manually. Mocks must be cleaned up to prevent leaking into other tests.
Move the time-dependent test to a separate file. Mocks are file-scoped and can't be restored within the same file.
Use jest.useFakeTimers() instead. Fake timers auto-restore between tests without manual cleanup.
Mock Date.now() at the module level (before all tests). Consistent mocking prevents inter-test conflicts.

questions.explanation: Always restore mocks after each test to prevent state leakage. jest.restoreAllMocks() in afterEach is the standard cleanup pattern.

questions.question: You test a form component by checking that document.querySelector('.submit-btn') exists after render. The test breaks when a colleague renames the CSS class. What's the more resilient approach?

options.text: Query by accessible role: screen.getByRole('button', { name: 'Submit' }). Testing Library's role-based queries test what users see, not implementation details like class names.
Use data-testid attributes: <button data-testid="submit-btn">. Test IDs are explicitly for testing and don't change with styling.
Use XPath selectors. XPath is stable across class changes because it traverses the DOM structure.
Snapshot test the entire component. Snapshots catch all changes, so class renames would be flagged in review.

questions.explanation: Testing Library encourages querying by role, label, or text — what users actually interact with. This makes tests resilient to implementation changes.

questions.question: Your Playwright E2E test clicks a button and immediately asserts the result. The test fails intermittently with 'element not found'. Running the test slowly (with a sleep) makes it pass. What's the proper fix?

options.text: Use Playwright's built-in auto-waiting: await expect(page.getByText('Result')).toBeVisible(). Playwright automatically retries assertions until the element appears or timeout. Never use arbitrary sleeps.
Add page.waitForTimeout(2000) before the assertion. A fixed delay ensures async operations complete.
Run the test with --slow-mo flag in CI. Slowing execution gives the browser time to render.
The button click is failing silently. Add a retry loop around the click: while (!clicked) { await button.click(); }.

questions.explanation: Playwright's expect automatically retries with configurable timeout. Fixed sleeps are flaky and slow. Auto-waiting is the correct E2E testing pattern.

questions.question: Your E2E tests take 20 minutes to run in CI. The suite has 200 tests running sequentially. Most tests are independent. How do you reduce the total time?

options.text: Run tests in parallel using Playwright's built-in sharding: npx playwright test --shard=1/4 across multiple CI jobs. Independent tests run simultaneously, reducing wall time to ~5 minutes.
Combine multiple assertions into single test cases. Fewer tests = faster suite.
Skip E2E tests in CI and run them locally before merge. E2E tests are too slow for CI.
Use headless: false in CI. Headed mode renders faster because GPU acceleration is available.

questions.explanation: Parallelization via sharding splits tests across CI workers. Independent tests run concurrently, cutting total time proportionally.

questions.question: You mock a service module in your test. The component under test imports the service via a re-export barrel file (index.ts). Your jest.mock('./services/userService') doesn't take effect — the real implementation runs. Why?

options.text: The component imports from './services' (barrel), not './services/userService'. jest.mock path must match the import path exactly. Mock './services' or mock the specific path the component uses.
Barrel files create deep copies of exports. Mocking the original module doesn't affect the barrel's copy.
jest.mock doesn't work with named exports. You need to mock the default export instead.
Re-exported modules can only be mocked with jest.spyOn, not jest.mock. SpyOn handles indirection.

questions.explanation: jest.mock must target the exact module path used in the import statement. Barrel file re-exports create a different module reference than the original.

questions.question:
You write tests that check exact HTML output: expect(container.innerHTML).toBe('

Title

'). Tests break constantly with whitespace or attribute order changes. How do you make these resilient?

options.text: Test behavior and accessibility, not HTML structure. Instead of matching HTML strings, assert: screen.getByRole('heading', { name: 'Title' }) exists. Test what the component does, not how it renders.
Use snapshot tests. Jest snapshots handle whitespace normalization automatically.
Normalize the HTML with a parser before comparison. This handles attribute order and whitespace differences.
Pin exact versions of all dependencies. HTML output changes come from library updates.

questions.explanation: Testing HTML strings couples tests to implementation. Test user-observable behavior (roles, text, interactions) to make tests resilient to refactoring.

questions.question: Your component test renders a <UserDashboard> that internally fetches data via useSWR. In tests, you mock the API response, but the component shows the loading spinner forever. The mock returns data instantly. What's likely wrong?

options.text: useSWR's cache might be returning stale data from a previous test, or the SWR provider isn't configured for testing. Wrap the test render in an SWRConfig with a fresh provider and dedupingInterval: 0 to ensure fresh fetches per test.
useSWR uses Web Workers internally. Test environments don't support Workers, so the fetch never resolves.
Mock must return a Promise, not raw data. useSWR rejects synchronous responses.
React Testing Library's render is synchronous. useSWR's async fetch needs waitFor around the render call itself.

questions.explanation: SWR caches and deduplicates requests. In tests, stale cache or deduplication prevents fresh mocked responses. Reset the SWR cache/provider between tests.

questions.question: You use React Context to share a theme value ('light'/'dark') across components. It works, but now you want to also share user data and notification count via the same context. A colleague warns this will cause performance issues. Why?

options.text: Every context consumer re-renders when ANY value in the context changes. Putting unrelated data in one context means a notification count update re-renders every themed component. Split into separate contexts.
React Context has a 10KB size limit. Adding more data exceeds the limit and triggers fallback rendering.
Context values are serialized to JSON on every update. Large objects cause serialization bottlenecks.
Multiple values in context create circular references that prevent garbage collection.

questions.explanation: Context triggers re-render for all consumers on any value change. Unrelated data in one context causes unnecessary renders. Use separate contexts for separate concerns.

questions.question: You create a context provider at the app root and a deeply nested component consumes it. Between them are 15 intermediate components. Do those 15 components need to pass the context value as props?

options.text: No — that's the whole point of Context. It eliminates prop drilling. Any descendant can consume the context directly with useContext, regardless of nesting depth. Intermediate components don't need to know about it.
Yes — Context still uses props internally. You must pass a contextRef prop through each intermediate component.
No, but all 15 intermediate components re-render when the context value changes because they're inside the provider.
No, but Context has a maximum depth of 10 component levels. Beyond that, you need a second provider.

questions.explanation: Context provides direct access to values without prop drilling. Only components that call useContext for that context re-render on changes.

questions.question: You dispatch an action in Redux but the component doesn't update. You check the Redux DevTools and see the state DID change in the store. The component uses useSelector. What's the most common cause?

options.text: The reducer is mutating state directly instead of returning a new object. useSelector uses reference equality — mutated objects keep the same reference, so React doesn't detect a change.
useSelector has a one-frame delay. The component updates on the next render cycle. Add forceUpdate() after dispatch.
Redux DevTools shows pending state, not committed state. The actual store hasn't updated yet.
useSelector only works with string and number values. Object selectors need useStore() instead.
