questions.explanation: Mutating state = same reference = useSelector thinks nothing changed. Always return new objects from reducers. Redux Toolkit's Immer helps prevent this.

questions.question: You fetch user data with useEffect + useState. When the user navigates away and back, the data re-fetches from scratch — showing a loading spinner again for data they just saw. What library pattern solves this?

options.text: Use React Query (TanStack Query) or SWR. They cache server data by query key. When re-visiting, cached data displays instantly while revalidating in the background. No spinner for previously fetched data.
Store the fetched data in localStorage. Check localStorage before fetching to show cached data immediately.
Move the fetch to a parent component that doesn't unmount. Child components receive data as props.
Use React.memo to prevent the component from unmounting on navigation. Memoized components persist across route changes.

questions.explanation: React Query / SWR provide intelligent server state caching, background revalidation, and stale-while-revalidate behavior out of the box.

questions.question: Your app has a complex form with many interdependent validations (e.g., end date must be after start date, total must equal sum of line items). useState with multiple setState calls is getting tangled. What pattern helps?

options.text: useReducer with a well-defined action/state machine. All form state transitions go through a single reducer, making interdependent validations explicit and testable in one place.
Use useEffect to watch each field and trigger validation. Effects handle cross-field dependencies automatically.
Separate each field into its own useState and validate in the onChange handler. Isolated state prevents tangling.
Use a global Redux store for form state. Redux's middleware can intercept and validate every action.

questions.explanation: useReducer centralizes state transitions, making complex interdependent logic explicit and testable. It's the React-native solution for complex state.

questions.question: Your Redux store has a single large slice with 50 fields. Every action triggers re-renders in all connected components, even those that only use 1-2 fields. What's the architectural fix?

options.text: Split into multiple slices (user, ui, data, etc.) and write granular selectors. useSelector only re-renders when the selected slice of state changes — selecting the whole state defeats this optimization.
Add React.memo to all connected components. Memo prevents re-renders regardless of selector granularity.
Use shallowEqual as the second argument to useSelector. This compares individual fields instead of the whole object.
Redux can't optimize re-renders at the selector level. Migrate to Zustand for better render performance.

questions.explanation: Granular selectors and split slices minimize re-renders. useSelector with a narrow selector only triggers re-render when that specific data changes.

questions.question: You use React Query to fetch a list of items. After creating a new item via a mutation, the list still shows the old data. The mutation succeeds. How do you update the cached list?

options.text: Call queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['items'] }) in the mutation's onSuccess. This marks the cached list as stale and triggers a refetch, showing the new item.
React Query automatically detects new data on the server and refreshes all caches. The issue is the mutation response format.
Call queryClient.clear() to wipe all caches. This forces every query to refetch from scratch.
Add the new item to the list in local state. React Query caches are read-only and can't be invalidated.

questions.explanation: invalidateQueries marks specific cached queries as stale, triggering refetch. This is the standard React Query mutation/cache coordination pattern.

questions.question: You build a settings panel with Context. Changing any setting re-renders 50 unrelated components that consume the same context. React.memo on consumers doesn't help. Why not?

options.text: React.memo checks props, but context changes bypass memo — useContext always triggers a re-render when the context value changes. Split the context into separate providers or use a state library that supports granular subscriptions.
React.memo doesn't work with functional components. It only works with class components' shouldComponentUpdate.
The context value object has 50 fields. React.memo can only shallow-compare objects with fewer than 10 keys.
Context updates don't use React's reconciliation. They directly mutate the DOM, bypassing memo checks.

questions.explanation: Context bypasses React.memo. Any useContext consumer re-renders when the provider value changes. Split contexts or use libraries like Zustand/Jotai for selective subscriptions.

questions.question: You have a multi-step wizard form. Step 3 needs data from Step 1 and Step 2, but each step is a separate component/route. Where should the form state live?

options.text: Lift state to the wizard parent component or use a dedicated store (Zustand/context). Each step reads from and writes to the shared state. On final submit, all data is available in one place.
Pass data as URL query parameters between steps. This preserves state across navigation without lifting state.
Each step stores its data in localStorage. On the final step, aggregate all localStorage entries.
Use useRef in each step component. Refs persist across renders and can be read from sibling components.

questions.explanation: Multi-step forms need shared state above all steps. Zustand/context/parent state ensures each step can read and update the full form data.

questions.question: You implement optimistic updates with React Query — updating the UI before the server confirms. The mutation fails, but the UI doesn't revert. Your onError callback runs. What's missing?

options.text: You need to save the previous cache data in onMutate (via queryClient.getQueryData), then in onError restore it with queryClient.setQueryData. Also invalidate in onSettled to sync with server truth regardless of outcome.
React Query automatically rolls back optimistic updates on error. Check that your API returns the correct error status code.
Call queryClient.resetQueries() in onError. This reverts all queries to their initial state.
Optimistic updates can't be rolled back. Show an error toast and let the user manually refresh.

questions.explanation: Optimistic update pattern: snapshot data in onMutate, update cache optimistically, restore snapshot in onError, invalidate in onSettled for truth reconciliation.

questions.question: Your Redux app has middleware that logs every action. You notice dispatching a single user action triggers 15 actions (cascading dispatches from other middleware and thunks). Performance degrades. What's the pattern anti-pattern here?

options.text: Action cascading — one action triggering many others — creates unpredictable flows and N re-renders. Batch related state changes into a single action handled by one reducer, or use Redux Toolkit's createAsyncThunk which dispatches lifecycle actions predictably.
15 actions is normal for complex apps. The issue is the logger middleware — it's the bottleneck. Remove logging in production.
Redux batches all synchronous dispatches into one re-render by default. The 15 actions cause only 1 render — the issue is elsewhere.
Use Redux Saga instead of thunks. Sagas run in a separate thread and don't cascade actions on the main thread.

questions.explanation: Action cascading makes state flow unpredictable and causes excessive renders. Consolidate related state changes into atomic actions or use batch() for synchronous dispatches.

questions.question: You build a collaborative editor where multiple users edit simultaneously. Local state updates must be instant (no server round-trip lag) but also sync with the server and other users' changes. What state architecture handles this?

options.text: Local-first architecture with CRDTs (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types) or operational transforms. Apply local changes immediately, sync changes over WebSocket, and merge concurrent edits automatically without conflicts.
Use React Query with a 100ms polling interval. Fast polling approximates real-time collaboration.
Redux with WebSocket middleware. Dispatch actions to both local store and server. Server broadcasts to other clients who replay actions.
Lock the document section being edited. Only one user can edit at a time — this prevents all conflicts.

questions.explanation: CRDTs enable conflict-free concurrent editing by mathematically guaranteeing merge consistency. Local-first means instant UI; sync handles eventual consistency.

questions.question: Your fetch request to a different domain fails with 'Access-Control-Allow-Origin' error in the console. The same endpoint works fine in Postman. Why does it fail in the browser?

options.text: Browsers enforce CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing). Requests to different origins need the server to include Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. Postman doesn't enforce CORS because it's not a browser.
The browser blocks HTTP requests to HTTPS servers. Postman doesn't check protocol mismatch.
Browsers have a built-in API whitelist. Only approved domains can be fetched. Register your domain with the browser vendor.
The browser's firewall blocks cross-domain requests. Disable the firewall in browser settings.

questions.explanation: CORS is a browser security mechanism. The server must whitelist the requesting origin via response headers. Non-browser clients like Postman bypass CORS entirely.

questions.question: Your app sends a PUT request to update a user, but the server receives an empty body. The request works with POST. The code is identical except for the HTTP method. What's likely wrong?

options.text: You're likely not setting Content-Type: application/json header, or not calling JSON.stringify on the body. Some servers reject PUT bodies without explicit content type, while being lenient with POST.
PUT requests don't support request bodies in the HTTP spec. PUT only sends the URL — use PATCH for body content.
The browser preflight (OPTIONS) for PUT is rejecting the body. POST doesn't trigger preflight because it's a 'simple' method.
PUT is idempotent, so the browser caches the first empty request and replays it. Clear the browser cache.

questions.explanation: Missing Content-Type header causes some servers to ignore the body. Always set Content-Type: application/json and JSON.stringify the body for PUT/PATCH/POST.

questions.question: You fetch a user profile via REST and get back 50 fields, but your UI only displays name and avatar. A colleague suggests GraphQL. What specific problem would GraphQL solve here?

options.text: Over-fetching. GraphQL lets the client specify exactly which fields to return: query { user { name avatar } }. You get only the 2 fields needed, reducing payload and bandwidth.
GraphQL compresses response data automatically. The same 50 fields would transfer faster because of built-in gzip.
GraphQL uses binary protocol instead of JSON. Smaller payloads are a side effect of the format, not field selection.
GraphQL caches on the server, so repeated requests for the same fields are instant. REST servers don't cache.

questions.explanation: GraphQL's core advantage is precise field selection. The client requests exactly what it needs — no over-fetching (too much data) or under-fetching (too little).

questions.question: You store a JWT in localStorage for authentication. A security auditor flags this as a vulnerability. What's the risk?

options.text: localStorage is accessible to any JavaScript on the page. An XSS attack (injected script) can steal the JWT and impersonate the user. httpOnly cookies prevent JS access.
localStorage has a 5MB limit. JWTs can exceed this and be silently truncated, causing auth failures.
localStorage is shared across tabs. Multiple tabs sending the same JWT causes server-side rate limiting.
JWTs in localStorage expire faster. The browser clears localStorage tokens after 30 minutes.

questions.explanation: XSS + localStorage = token theft. httpOnly cookies are inaccessible to JavaScript, protecting tokens even if XSS exists. Defense in depth.

questions.question: Your WebSocket connection drops when the user's network briefly disconnects (e.g., switching WiFi). The app shows stale data with no indication of disconnection. What's the robust pattern?

options.text: Implement reconnection with exponential backoff, show a disconnected state in the UI, and re-sync missed data on reconnect. Listen for the WebSocket close event and attempt reconnection after increasing delays.
Use a heartbeat ping every 100ms. If a ping fails, refresh the entire page to re-establish the connection.
WebSocket connections auto-reconnect. The browser handles reconnection transparently. Your issue is the server closing idle connections.
Switch to Server-Sent Events. SSE maintains persistent connections that survive network switches.

questions.explanation: WebSocket has no built-in reconnection. Implement it manually: detect close, backoff retry, show UI feedback, and re-sync missed messages on reconnect.

questions.question: Your dashboard makes 20 API calls on page load — one for each widget. Time to Interactive is 6 seconds. Each call takes ~300ms. How do you optimize the loading pattern?

options.text: Use Promise.all() to fire all 20 requests concurrently, load critical above-the-fold widgets first, and lazy-load below-the-fold widgets on scroll. Consider a BFF (Backend for Frontend) that aggregates calls server-side.
Chain the requests sequentially with error handling between each. This prevents server overload from 20 simultaneous connections.
Combine all 20 widgets into a single API endpoint. Always fetch everything in one call regardless of what's visible.
Cache all widget data in IndexedDB and never refetch. Show stale data permanently for instant loading.

questions.explanation: Concurrent requests via Promise.all, prioritization of visible content, and optional server-side aggregation dramatically reduce perceived and actual load time.

questions.question: Your GraphQL query fetches a list of orders, and for each order fetches the customer, and for each customer fetches their address. The page is slow. The backend team says your query causes an N+1 problem. What does that mean?

options.text: For N orders, the server makes 1 query for orders + N queries for customers + N queries for addresses. Each nested resolver fires a separate DB query. Backend needs DataLoader to batch these into bulk queries.
N+1 means the query returns N+1 results instead of N. GraphQL adds an extra root node that the frontend must filter out.
N+1 is a frontend caching issue. Apollo Client makes N+1 cache lookups per render. Increase cache size to fix.
The query has too many nesting levels. GraphQL has a depth limit of N, and your query exceeds it by 1.

questions.explanation: N+1: 1 query for the list, then N individual queries per item for related data. DataLoader batches these into 1 list query + 1 batch query — drastically reducing DB load.

questions.question: Your app uses JWTs that expire after 15 minutes. Users complain about being logged out mid-session. You don't want long-lived access tokens. What's the standard pattern?

options.text: Use a refresh token stored in an httpOnly cookie. Before the access token expires, silently exchange the refresh token for a new access token. Users stay logged in without long-lived access tokens.
Extend the JWT expiry to 24 hours. Short-lived tokens cause poor UX and aren't necessary for most apps.
Store the JWT in sessionStorage instead of localStorage. sessionStorage tokens don't expire during the browser session.
Catch 401 errors and redirect to login. Periodic re-authentication is the most secure pattern.

questions.explanation: Refresh tokens enable short-lived access tokens (security) with seamless re-authentication (UX). The refresh token has a longer lifespan and is rotated on use.

questions.question: Your WebSocket server sends messages faster than the client can process them. The browser tab becomes unresponsive. Messages pile up in the WebSocket buffer. What's the solution?

options.text: Implement backpressure: throttle processing on the client, and/or have the server respect client capacity by reducing send rate. Batch messages or use a message queue with the client pulling at its own pace.
Increase the browser's WebSocket buffer size via a meta tag. Default buffer is too small for high-throughput applications.
Move WebSocket handling to a Web Worker. Workers have unlimited buffer and never block the main thread.
Use multiple WebSocket connections. Each connection has its own buffer, distributing the message load.

questions.explanation: Backpressure controls the flow of data to prevent overwhelming the consumer. Throttle client processing, batch updates, or signal the server to slow down.

questions.question: Users report that rapidly clicking a 'Submit Order' button creates duplicate orders. The button is disabled after click, but sometimes two requests arrive at the server before the UI disables. How do you prevent duplicates at the API level?

options.text: Use an idempotency key: generate a unique ID client-side and send it with the request. The server stores the key and returns the existing result for duplicate keys instead of creating a new order.
Add a 500ms debounce on the click handler. This ensures only one request per click regardless of speed.
Use HTTP PUT instead of POST. PUT is idempotent by spec — servers automatically deduplicate PUT requests.
Lock the JavaScript event loop during the fetch. This prevents any other clicks from firing until the request completes.

questions.explanation: Idempotency keys are the standard pattern for preventing duplicate submissions. Client-side disabling is a UX measure, not a guarantee — server-side dedup is required.

questions.question: Your public GraphQL API is being abused — attackers send deeply nested queries like { user { friends { friends { friends { ... } } } } } that overwhelm the server. REST APIs don't have this problem. How do you protect GraphQL?

options.text: Implement query depth limiting, query complexity analysis, and query cost calculation. Reject queries exceeding a depth of N or a complexity score threshold. Also consider persisted queries that whitelist approved operations.
Add rate limiting by IP address. This prevents any single client from overwhelming the server.
Disable introspection in production. Without the schema, attackers can't craft nested queries.
Switch to GraphQL subscriptions. Subscriptions use WebSocket and can't be nested.

questions.explanation: GraphQL's flexibility enables abuse. Depth limiting, complexity analysis, and persisted queries are defense layers. Rate limiting alone doesn't prevent a single expensive query.

questions.question: Your SPA sends requests to your API on a different subdomain. You use httpOnly cookies for auth, but the browser doesn't send them cross-origin. The cookie is set correctly on the auth domain. What's misconfigured?

options.text: Cross-origin requests need credentials: 'include' in fetch AND the server must set Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true with a specific origin (not wildcard *). The cookie also needs SameSite=None; Secure.
httpOnly cookies can only be sent to the exact domain that set them. Subdomains are different origins. Move the API to the same domain.
Cookies don't work with fetch(). Use XMLHttpRequest which has native cookie support.
The browser blocks cookies larger than 4KB. Your JWT might exceed this and be silently dropped.

questions.explanation: Cross-origin cookie sending requires: credentials: 'include', CORS with specific origin and Allow-Credentials, and SameSite=None; Secure on the cookie.

questions.question: Your E2E test needs to verify that a toast notification appears and disappears after 3 seconds. The test passes locally but fails in CI where the toast has already vanished by the time the assertion runs. What's the correct approach?

options.text: Assert the toast is visible immediately after the triggering action using auto-waiting: await expect(page.getByText('Saved')).toBeVisible(). Then assert it disappears: await expect(page.getByText('Saved')).not.toBeVisible({ timeout: 5000 }).
Add a page.waitForTimeout(1000) before asserting. This gives the toast time to render but checks before it disappears.
Mock the toast timer to prevent it from disappearing. Tests should control time to avoid race conditions.
Take a screenshot during the test and visually verify the toast is in the image. Visual assertions don't have timing issues.

questions.explanation: Playwright's auto-waiting retries assertions until they pass or timeout. Assert visibility immediately (auto-waits for appearance), then assert disappearance with sufficient timeout.

questions.question: You import a utility library that's 200KB. You only use one function from it. Your bundle analyzer shows the entire 200KB in the final bundle. Why?

options.text: The library doesn't support tree-shaking — likely uses CommonJS (module.exports) instead of ES modules (export). CommonJS can't be statically analyzed, so the bundler includes everything.
Tree-shaking only removes unused files, not unused exports from a file. One import means the whole file is included.
Webpack disables tree-shaking by default. Add treeShake: true to the config.
The function you use internally imports other parts of the library. Transitive dependencies always include the full package.

questions.explanation: Tree-shaking requires ES module syntax (import/export) for static analysis. CommonJS is dynamic and can't be statically analyzed for dead code elimination.

questions.question: Your production build takes 45 seconds. During development, every code change also takes 45 seconds to reflect in the browser. A colleague suggests using Vite instead of Webpack. What would change?

options.text: Vite serves ES modules directly in development — no bundling needed. Changes are reflected via Hot Module Replacement in milliseconds. Production still bundles with Rollup, but dev experience is dramatically faster.
Vite uses Rust-based compilation that's 100x faster than Webpack's JavaScript-based pipeline for both dev and production.
Vite skips bundling entirely, even in production. Modern browsers handle thousands of unbundled modules efficiently.
Vite pre-compiles everything on startup. The 45-second build happens once, then all changes are instant because it works from cache.

questions.explanation: Vite's dev server uses native ES modules — no bundling step. Changes go through HMR in ms. Production uses Rollup for optimized bundles.

questions.question: You write modern JavaScript with optional chaining (?.) and nullish coalescing (??). The code works in Chrome but crashes in an older company browser. What tool solves this?

options.text: Babel transpiles modern syntax to equivalent older JavaScript. It transforms ?. and ?? into if-checks and ternaries that all browsers understand. Configure the target browsers in browserslist.
Add a polyfill script tag. Polyfills patch all missing syntax features at runtime.
Set type="module" on the script tag. Module scripts automatically downcompile for older browsers.
Use TypeScript instead. TypeScript always outputs ES5-compatible code that works everywhere.

questions.explanation: Babel is a JavaScript compiler that converts modern syntax to older equivalents. Polyfills add missing APIs; Babel transforms syntax — they're complementary.

questions.question: A developer commits code with a lint error. The team doesn't catch it until the PR is reviewed 2 days later. How do you prevent lint errors from being committed?

options.text: Add a pre-commit hook using husky + lint-staged. It runs the linter on staged files before allowing the commit. Errors block the commit automatically, catching issues at the source.
Add a lint step in CI. CI rejects PRs with lint errors before they can be merged.
Configure VS Code to auto-fix on save. All developers using VS Code will have clean code.
Schedule a daily cron job to run the linter and email developers about their errors.

questions.explanation: Pre-commit hooks catch issues before they enter the repository. husky + lint-staged is the standard setup — it runs linting only on staged files for speed.

questions.question: Your TypeScript project compiles with tsc for type checking and also with Babel for transpilation. You add a TypeScript-only feature (const enum) and Babel can't compile it. Why?

options.text: Babel only strips type annotations — it doesn't understand TypeScript's type system. const enum requires type-level evaluation to inline values, which Babel can't do. Use regular enum or switch to SWC/esbuild which handle TS natively.
const enum is deprecated in TypeScript 5+. Babel updated to reject deprecated features.
Babel requires a specific plugin for enums: @babel/plugin-transform-enums. Install it and const enum will work.
Babel and tsc can't coexist in the same project. Choose one transpiler and use it exclusively.

questions.explanation: Babel does per-file transpilation by stripping types. Features requiring cross-file type information (const enum, namespace merging) need a full TS compiler.

questions.question: You add dynamic imports for route-based code splitting. The build produces 50 tiny chunk files (1-5KB each). Page load now makes 50 HTTP requests. What's the trade-off and fix?

options.text: Too many small chunks = HTTP overhead (even with HTTP/2). Configure the bundler's minChunkSize or manualChunks to merge small chunks into larger ones. Group related routes into shared chunks.
50 chunks is optimal. HTTP/2 multiplexing handles unlimited parallel requests with zero overhead.
Dynamic imports always create one chunk per import. Switch to static imports to control chunk count.
Use a CDN. CDNs serve small files faster than large ones, making 50 small chunks faster than fewer large ones.

questions.explanation: Many tiny chunks create overhead despite HTTP/2. Configure chunk strategy to balance code splitting benefits against request overhead. Merge small related chunks.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline installs dependencies, lints, type-checks, runs tests, and builds — all sequentially. Total: 12 minutes. Lint and type-check are independent of each other. How do you optimize?

options.text: Run lint and type-check in parallel (they're independent). Cache node_modules keyed on lockfile hash. Use incremental TypeScript builds. This can cut the pipeline from 12 to ~6 minutes.
Skip linting in CI — rely on pre-commit hooks. This removes one step entirely.
Use a faster CI provider. Build time is primarily determined by the CI runner's CPU speed.
Combine lint, type-check, and test into a single command. Fewer steps = less overhead from step initialization.

questions.explanation: Parallelize independent steps, cache dependencies, and use incremental builds. These are standard CI optimization techniques that significantly reduce pipeline time.

questions.question: Your monorepo has 5 packages. Running 'npm install' at the root installs dependencies for all packages, but shared dependencies are duplicated across each package's node_modules. How do you fix this?

options.text: Use a workspace-aware package manager (npm workspaces, pnpm, or Yarn workspaces). These hoist shared dependencies to the root node_modules and symlink packages, deduplicating shared deps.
Manually move shared dependencies to the root package.json. Child packages inherit root dependencies automatically.
Use npm dedupe after install. This command merges all duplicate dependencies into a single copy.
Install dependencies in Docker. Container layers share identical node_modules across packages.

questions.explanation: Workspaces manage monorepo dependencies properly — hoisting shared deps, linking local packages, and ensuring consistent versions across packages.

questions.question: Your monorepo build takes 15 minutes. You change one file in one package, but the build rebuilds all 5 packages. How do you make builds incremental?

options.text: Use Turborepo or Nx for task orchestration. They compute a dependency graph, cache build outputs by content hash, and only rebuild packages affected by changes. Unchanged packages use cached results.
Add a Makefile that checks file timestamps. Make only rebuilds targets with newer dependencies.
Build each package in a separate CI job triggered by path filters. Only the changed package's job runs.
Switch to a polyrepo. Monorepos can't support incremental builds because packages share dependencies.

questions.explanation: Turborepo/Nx provide content-hash-based caching. If a package's inputs haven't changed, the cached output is reused — turning 15-minute builds into seconds.

questions.question: You migrate from Babel to SWC for faster builds. Most code works, but a decorator-heavy feature breaks. Babel's @babel/plugin-proposal-decorators with legacy: true produced different output than SWC's decorator implementation. What's the root issue?

options.text: Decorators have multiple competing proposals (legacy/stage 2/TC39 2023). Babel's 'legacy' mode emits TypeScript-experimental-style decorators while SWC may default to a different proposal. Align the decorator proposal version in SWC config.
SWC doesn't support decorators at all. It's a known limitation of Rust-based transpilers.
Decorators are runtime features. The transpiler difference doesn't matter — the issue is a Node.js version mismatch.
SWC minifies decorator metadata by default. Disable minification to preserve decorator behavior.

questions.explanation: Decorator proposals went through multiple incompatible iterations. Babel and SWC may default to different versions. Ensure both use the same proposal spec.

questions.question: Your CI caches node_modules keyed on package-lock.json hash. A developer updates a transitive dependency via npm audit fix, which changes package-lock.json. CI creates a new cache entry but the build fails with 'cannot find module X'. The old cache worked fine. Why?

options.text: The new lockfile is correct, but CI restored the old cache first (before npm install ran), then npm install partially updated node_modules on top of stale modules. Old artifacts + new lockfile = inconsistency. Clear cache on lockfile changes or use npm ci which deletes node_modules first.
npm audit fix can introduce breaking changes in transitive dependencies. The missing module was removed in the updated version.
CI caches are immutable. The old cache entry corrupted the new one because they share the same storage bucket.
package-lock.json hash changes invalidate the TypeScript cache too. Stale .d.ts files reference the old module paths.

questions.explanation: npm install on top of cached stale node_modules can leave orphaned or conflicting packages. npm ci deletes node_modules and does a clean install from the lockfile.

questions.question: You publish an npm library. Users report it dramatically increases their bundle size. Your library imports lodash, moment, and a UI framework internally. What's the library authoring mistake?

options.text: These should be peerDependencies, not dependencies, and your library should be published as ES modules with sideEffects: false in package.json. This enables tree-shaking in consumers' bundlers. Also, import specific lodash functions instead of the whole library.
Bundle size is the consumer's responsibility. They should configure their own tree-shaking.
Mark the library as type: module in package.json. This automatically enables tree-shaking for all consumers.
Use npm pack --production to exclude devDependencies from the published bundle.

questions.explanation: Library authors must: use ESM exports, mark sideEffects: false, use peerDependencies for large shared deps, and import specific submodules. These enable consumer tree-shaking.
